profile.behavior: Inquisitive, acrobatic foliage-gleaner; often hangs upside down while taking insects from leaves and bark. Typically forages in social flocks outside the breeding season and carries food to a perch to handle it.
field_marks: Tiny, round-bodied bird with a large head and short neck
Black cap and neat black throat bib
Stark white cheeks separating cap and bib
Soft gray back, wings, and long narrow tail
Short, pointed dark bill; dark eyes and gray legs
profile.habitat: Deciduous and mixed woodlands, swamps and streamside woods, open woods, parks, and tree-rich suburban or urban settings.

sources.title: Carolina Chickadee Identification — Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Carolina Chickadee Life History — Cornell Lab of Ornithology

findings: Verified the identity as Carolina Chickadee (Poecile carolinensis), family Paridae. The plate’s stated length, wingspan, and weight exactly match both independent sources.

The depicted bird has one head, one short dark beak, two legs with appropriately grasping feet, one long narrow tail, and two wings (the far wing is naturally occluded in the side view). Plumage and proportions agree with the reference photos: black cap and bib, stark white cheeks, soft-gray back/wings/tail, pale underparts, and a round-bodied, large-headed silhouette.

All visible factual text is supported: habitat, acrobatic foliage-gleaning, upside-down feeding, winter/social flocking, and carrying food to a perch are consistent with the Cornell and Audubon accounts. The plate contains no place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation information.

Minor non-material limitation: Carolina and Black-capped Chickadees can be difficult to separate visually; the plate does not state Carolina’s relatively more restricted bib and reduced white wing edging. Its illustrated bib and gray wing edging nevertheless remain consistent with Carolina Chickadee.
